title: Voluntary Food Climate Labeling Act
plain_summary: This bill lets the Environmental Protection Agency create a new program that allows food companies to put special climate labels on their products. These labels would show customers how much pollution was created when making that food. Food companies do not have to join this program, but if they choose to, they must follow specific rules and get approval from the EPA first. The labels would help people know which foods are better for the environment when they go shopping.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Voluntary Food Climate Labeling Act.

sections.title: Section 2  Voluntary Food Climate Labeling Program
sections.text: The head of the Environmental Protection Agency (the government office that protects the environment) must create a program that lets food companies put special climate labels on their products if they want to. The agency head will work with the Secretary of Agriculture and the Federal Trade Commission to set up and run this program. Food companies cannot put these special climate labels on their food unless the Environmental Protection Agency gives them permission first. Companies that want to use these labels must fill out an application. The agency will look at each application and approve it if the company promises to follow the program rules. The agency will not judge whether the food is actually good or bad for the environment when deciding to approve applications. If a company gets approved, the agency can tell them exactly where to put the label on their food or food packaging. The agency head must decide what the climate label will look like, what information it will show, and how companies must prove their information is correct. When making these decisions, the agency must talk with people from the food industry. The agency can set up a special committee or use a special process called negotiated rulemaking to get input from food industry experts. The climate label must show information to shoppers in a way that helps them make food buying choices. The label cannot say any food is good or bad, but must give shoppers numbers about the food's environmental impact. The label must use words, numbers, and pictures chosen by the agency. The label must show that the Environmental Protection Agency has checked the information. The label must include a logo so shoppers can recognize it. The label must include a QR code that shoppers can scan with their phones to get more detailed information. The climate label must show two sets of numbers about greenhouse gases (gases that contribute to climate change) released during the entire life of the food and its ingredients. The first set of numbers covers greenhouse gases released during growing ingredients, making non-food materials used in production, processing the food, packaging it, and getting it to stores. The second set of numbers covers greenhouse gases released during storing the food, displaying it in stores, consumer use including energy to store food at home, and disposing of the food and packaging at the end of its life. The food company is responsible for making sure the first set of numbers is accurate. The Environmental Protection Agency is responsible for making sure the second set of numbers is accurate, and will work with the food company and advisory committee to develop these numbers. The QR code must give access to information about any voluntary promises the company has made to reduce greenhouse gases, and to a database the agency creates. The agency must set rules for how companies prove their information is correct. These rules must include following uniform standards for collecting and analyzing information, using entities certified by the agency to measure and verify information, and using the best available scientific information. When creating these rules, the agency must consider international standards for measuring carbon emissions throughout a product's life, including ISO standards and protocols from the Greenhouse Gas Protocol program. The agency must make sure its verification method matches current best practices used by the food industry. Within two years of this law being passed, the agency must create a program that lets companies voluntarily promise to reduce greenhouse gas emissions from their labeled foods and report information about how sustainable their practices are. Within two years of this law being passed, the agency must create a public database that anyone can access for free. This database will explain the program's goals and methods, show the greenhouse gas information for each food in the program, include voluntary promises and sustainability information from companies, and provide other sustainability information about labeled foods. Seven years after this law passes, and every five years after that, the agency must publish a report on how well the program works at giving useful information to consumers and reducing greenhouse gas emissions from food. The agency must also give Congress recommendations for new laws that could make the program work better. Within two years of this law being passed, the agency must finalize rules for running the program. Seven years after this law passes, and every five years after that, the agency must update these rules to make the program more effective. The agency must create a technical assistance board within the Environmental Protection Agency to help entities that want to participate in the program, entities that verify information, entities making voluntary commitments, and retail food stores. This board will include subject matter experts, academics, trade associations, consultants, Department of Agriculture leaders, and industry representatives. Within three years of this law being passed, the agency must create a program to teach consumers about the labeling program by giving educational materials to food stores and reaching the public through announcements and advertising. Any person who violates the requirements of this law can be fined up to ten thousand dollars for each violation. Each separate violation with a specific type of food counts as a separate offense. If someone continues to violate an agency order, each day of continued violation counts as a separate offense. Federal courts can issue orders to stop violations and provide other appropriate legal remedies. The law defines key terms including Administrator (the head of the Environmental Protection Agency), applicant (a person who makes, imports, distributes, or sells food and has applied to participate), food industry stakeholders (various entities involved in growing, processing, selling, or studying food), greenhouse gas (various gases including carbon dioxide and methane that contribute to climate change), greenhouse gas emission (the release of these gases into the air), labeling program (the voluntary program created by this law), open license (a legal tool that lets people freely access and use information), and participant (a person authorized to put the climate label on food).
